title: Introduction
content:
Introduction
UIGen is a runtime frontend generator for OpenAPI-described APIs. Point it at any OpenAPI 3.x or Swagger 2.0 spec and it serves a fully functional, interactive frontend: tables, forms, detail views, authentication, search, and more, with zero boilerplate and zero code to write.
What UIGen does
UIGen reads your API spec and generates a complete UI at runtime:
List views: paginated, sortable tables for every collection endpoint
Detail views: read-only record pages with related resource links
Create / edit forms: validated forms derived from request body schemas
Search: global and per-resource filtered search using query parameters from the spec
Dashboard: auto-generated overview with resource counts and quick links
Multi-step wizards: triggered automatically for large or nested forms
Authentication: Bearer token, API Key, HTTP Basic, and credential-based login, all auto-detected from 
All API calls are live (UIGen proxies requests to your real backend, so there is no mocking and no stub data).
Who it's for
UIGen is useful for:
API developers who want an instant admin UI or internal tool without building a frontend
Teams that need a quick way to explore and test a new API
Prototypers who want to demo an API to stakeholders without writing UI code
How it works
UIGen parses your spec into an Intermediate Representation (IR), then uses that IR to drive a React SPA. The CLI injects the IR into the app at startup and starts a local server.
See How It Works for a deeper look at the data flow.
Packages
UIGen ships as three packages:
Next steps
Quick Start: run UIGen in under a minute
Installation: install the packages that fit your use case

title: Quick Start
content:
Quick Start
The fastest way to get started with UIGen is using the  command (no installation required).
Step 1: Initialize a project
Create a new UIGen project with a single command:
You'll be prompted for:
Path to your existing OpenAPI spec (optional)
Whether to initialize a git repository
Or skip prompts with the  flag:
You should see output like:
Step 2: Start the development server
Navigate to your project and start the server:
The server will start at http://localhost:4400.
Step 3: Open the browser
Navigate to http://localhost:4400. UIGen serves a complete React SPA with:
A sidebar listing all resources detected from your spec
A dashboard overview with resource counts
Fully functional list, detail, create, edit, and delete views
Step 4: Navigate the generated UI
Click any resource in the sidebar to open its list view. From there you can:
Browse records fetched live from your API
Click a row to open the detail view
Use the New button to open the create form
Edit or delete records inline
Alternative: Quick test without init
If you just want to quickly test UIGen with an existing spec:
Or with a remote spec:
Customization
Use AI to Configure (Recommended)
The fastest way to configure UIGen is using AI agents:
Visualize with Config GUI (Optional)
Open the config GUI to see what AI generated and make manual adjustments:
Opens at http://localhost:4401 with tabs to visualize annotations, relationships, and styling.
Auto-annotate with AI
Use the included AI agent skill to automatically add UIGen annotations:
Open your AI agent (Kiro, Claude, ChatGPT, etc.)
Ask: "Use the auto-annotate skill to add UIGen annotations to my OpenAPI spec"
The agent will read  and intelligently annotate your spec
Customize the theme
AI can generate themes, or edit  manually:
Custom options
Custom port
Custom proxy base
If your API runs on a different host than what's declared in the spec's  field:
Verbose logging
Next steps
CLI Reference: init: all init command options
CLI Reference: serve: all serve command flags
CLI Reference: config: visualize AI-generated configuration
Authentication: how UIGen handles auth
Annotations: customize the generated UI

title: Installation
content:
Installation
UIGen ships as three separate packages. Install only what you need.
The CLI is the fastest way to get started. Use it to initialize projects, serve generated UIs, and configure annotations visually.
Or use it without installing via :
Use this when you want to spin up a UI for an existing API without writing any code, or when you want a complete project scaffold with configuration and AI agent skills.
CLI commands
- Initialize a new UIGen project with scaffolded structure
- Start a development server with the generated UI
- Open a visual GUI for managing annotations
The framework-agnostic core contains the adapters, IR types, and the  function. It has no UI dependencies.
Use this when you want to build your own renderer on top of UIGen's IR, or when you need to parse a spec programmatically.
The React renderer provides the full UI layer (views, forms, fields, auth, and layout) driven by the IR from .
Use this when you want to embed the generated UI inside an existing React application, or when you need to customise the rendering via the Override System.
Requirements
Node.js 18 or later
npm 9 or later (or pnpm / yarn equivalent)
Next steps
Quick Start: create a project and run UIGen in under a minute
CLI Reference: init: initialize new projects
CLI Reference: serve: all available flags for 
CLI Reference: config: visual configuration GUI
Override System: customise the generated UI

title: How It Works
content:
How It Works
UIGen transforms an API spec into a running frontend through a pipeline of discrete steps. Understanding this pipeline helps you know where to customise behaviour and how to debug unexpected output.
Data flow
Step 1: Adapter
The adapter reads the raw spec string and produces the IR. UIGen ships two adapters:
OpenAPI 3.x adapter: handles OpenAPI 3.0 and 3.1 documents
Swagger 2.0 adapter: handles Swagger 2.0 (formerly OpenAPI 2.0) documents
The adapter resolves all  references, detects view hints (list, detail, create, update, delete, search, wizard, action), infers pagination strategies, and extracts authentication schemes.
See Adapters for details.
Step 2: Intermediate Representation (IR)
The IR is a plain TypeScript object that describes the entire UI in framework-agnostic terms. It contains:
Resources: the entities in your API (e.g. , )
Operations: the HTTP endpoints for each resource, with view hints
Schema nodes: the field types, labels, validations, and UI hints for each field
Auth config: the authentication schemes and login endpoints
Dashboard config: widgets and resource counts for the overview page
The IR is serialised as JSON and injected into the React SPA as  at startup.
See Intermediate Representation for the full type definitions.
Step 3: React SPA
The React SPA reads the IR from  and renders the UI. It uses:
React Router for URL navigation and browser history
TanStack Query for data fetching, caching, and mutations
TanStack Table for sortable, paginated data tables
React Hook Form + Zod for form state and validation
shadcn/ui for accessible, pre-styled components
The SPA is served by the CLI's built-in HTTP server.
Step 4: API Proxy
All API calls from the SPA go through the CLI's built-in proxy at . The proxy:
Forwards requests to the target server (from the spec's  field, or )
Injects authentication headers transparently (Bearer token, API Key, HTTP Basic)
Strips UIGen-specific headers before forwarding
This means the SPA never makes direct cross-origin requests (the proxy handles CORS).
Two serving modes
The CLI operates in two modes depending on how it was installed:
Both modes expose the same URL and behaviour. The difference is internal.

title: Intermediate Representation
content:
Intermediate Representation
The Intermediate Representation (IR) is the central data model in UIGen. The adapter produces it from your spec, and the React SPA consumes it to render the UI. Because the IR is framework-agnostic, it can drive any renderer (React today, Svelte and Vue in the future).
The IR is defined in . The types below are sourced directly from that file.
The root object. Everything the SPA needs is here.
Basic metadata from the spec's  object.
Each entry in the spec's  array becomes a . The SPA uses these to populate the environment switcher.
A resource maps to a group of related endpoints (typically a REST resource like  or ).
An operation maps to a single HTTP endpoint. The  tells the renderer which view to use.
The view hint tells the renderer which UI pattern to use for an operation.
A  represents a single field or nested object in a request or response schema.
Authentication configuration extracted from the spec's .
The adapter detects the pagination strategy from query parameter names.
Relationships define connections between resources. They can be explicitly configured or automatically detected from API paths.
Relationship Types
*: One-to-many relationship (e.g., a user has many orders)
Path pattern: 
Example: 
: Many-to-one relationship (e.g., an order belongs to a user)
Path pattern: 
Example:  (from the order's perspective)
*: Many-to-many relationship (e.g., projects and tags)
Detected when symmetric relationships exist in both directions
Example:  and 
Explicit Type Configuration
Relationship types can be explicitly specified in the  file:
When the  field is present, it takes precedence over path-based derivation. If omitted, the type is automatically derived from the path pattern for backward compatibility.
Validation rules are extracted from JSON Schema constraints in the spec.

title: Adapters
content:
Adapters
An adapter reads a raw spec string and produces the Intermediate Representation (IR). UIGen ships two adapters, selected automatically based on the spec's format.
OpenAPI 3.x adapter
Handles OpenAPI 3.0 and 3.1 documents. This is the primary adapter and supports the full feature set.
The adapter processes:
: each path/method combination becomes an  with a 
: resolved and inlined into  trees
: mapped to  entries in 
: mapped to  entries for the environment switcher
: mapped to 
Swagger 2.0 adapter
Handles Swagger 2.0 (formerly OpenAPI 2.0) documents. The adapter normalises Swagger 2.0 constructs to the same IR shape:
→  equivalent
+  → 
→  equivalent
/  → 
The output IR is identical regardless of whether the input was OpenAPI 3.x or Swagger 2.0.
resolution
Both adapters perform full  resolution before building the IR. This means:
Circular references are detected and broken safely
Inline schemas and  schemas produce the same  output
Deeply nested  chains are fully resolved
Graceful degradation
Real-world specs are often incomplete or non-standard. The adapters are designed to degrade gracefully rather than fail:
Missing schemas: operations with no response schema produce a generic key-value 
Unknown field types: unmapped types fall back to 
Malformed operations: skipped with a ; recorded in 
Missing : defaults to 
Parsing errors are available in  for debugging.
Using the adapter directly
You can call  from  to parse a spec programmatically:
 auto-detects the spec format (OpenAPI 3.x vs Swagger 2.0) and selects the appropriate adapter.

title: Config Reconciliation
content:
Config Reconciliation
The Config Reconciliation System enables runtime merging of user-defined annotation overrides from  into your OpenAPI/Swagger specifications without modifying source files.
Why Config Reconciliation?
When using UIGen, you might want to customize how your API is rendered without modifying your original OpenAPI spec. Common scenarios include:
Hiding internal endpoints from the UI ()
Marking authentication endpoints ()
Customizing field labels ()
Overriding spec annotations for different environments
The config reconciliation system lets you define these overrides in a separate config file that takes precedence over your spec annotations.
Core Principles
Non-Destructive
Your source spec file remains unchanged on disk. All reconciliation happens in memory at runtime.
Config Takes Precedence
When an annotation exists in both your spec and config file, the config value wins.
Idempotent
Applying reconciliation twice produces the same result as applying it once.
Deterministic
Same input always produces the same output.
Config File Structure
Create a  file in your project root:
Element Path Syntax
Element paths identify where annotations should be applied:
Operations
Format: 
Examples:
Supported methods: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS, HEAD
Schema Properties
Format: 
Examples:
- top-level property
- top-level property
- nested property
- nested property
The reconciler automatically resolves  references.
How It Works
When you run , the reconciliation process:
Loads Config: Checks for  in your project root
Deep Clones Spec: Creates an in-memory copy of your source spec
Resolves Paths: Locates each element path in the spec
Merges Annotations: Applies config annotations (config takes precedence)
Validates: Ensures the reconciled spec is valid OpenAPI/Swagger
Generates UI: Uses the reconciled spec for IR generation
Your source spec file is never modified.
Generic Annotation Support
The reconciler treats all  annotations generically. This means:
New annotations work automatically without code changes
No hardcoded annotation names
Future annotations are supported out of the box
File Upload Configuration
Configure file upload restrictions using  and :
Array Syntax:  accepts an array of MIME types. You can use:
Specific types: , 
Wildcards: , , 
Multiple types: Mix and match as needed
File Size:  accepts a number in bytes:
1MB = 1048576 bytes
5MB = 5242880 bytes
10MB = 10485760 bytes
100MB = 104857600 bytes
Custom Annotations
Even if you create custom annotations, the reconciler will apply them correctly:
Relationship Configuration
UIGen can automatically detect relationships between resources based on API path patterns, but you can also explicitly configure relationships for better control and clarity.
Declaring Relationships
Add relationships to your config file:
Relationship Types
Three relationship types are supported:
- One-to-many relationship
Pattern: 
Example: A user has many orders
Path: 
Many-to-one relationship  
Pattern: 
Example: An order belongs to a user
Path:  (from order's perspective)
Many-to-many relationship
Detected when symmetric relationships exist
Example: Projects and tags
Paths:  and 
Explicit vs. Derived Types
Explicit types (recommended):
Derived types (backward compatible):
When the  field is present, it takes precedence over automatic derivation. If omitted, UIGen derives the type from the path pattern for backward compatibility.
Migration Tool
If you have existing relationships without explicit types, use the config-gui migration tool:
Open the config-gui in your browser
Navigate to the Relationships tab
Click "Migrate Now" in the banner
All relationships will be updated with explicit types
The migration preserves all existing relationship fields and only adds the  field.
Removing Annotations
Set an annotation to  to remove it from the reconciled spec:
This is different from setting it to :
Error Handling
Missing Config File
If  doesn't exist, UIGen uses your source spec without reconciliation. No errors.
Invalid Element Path
If an element path doesn't match any spec element:
Warning logged with suggestions
Annotation skipped
Reconciliation continues
Example:
Validation Failure
If the reconciled spec is invalid:
Error logged with details
Server exits (won't serve invalid spec)
Malformed Config
If the config file has syntax errors:
Error logged
Server exits with non-zero status
Integration with AI and Config GUI
The config system supports both AI-generated and manually-edited configurations:
AI Workflow (Recommended)
AI agents write directly to :
Tell AI what you want: "Hide password fields, Product.categoryId is FK to Category"
AI writes to 
Refresh browser to see changes applied via reconciliation
Config GUI Workflow (Visualization)
The config GUI reads and writes :
Open the config GUI in your browser
Visualize what AI generated or make manual adjustments
Save your changes
The config file is updated, and on next server restart, your changes are applied via reconciliation.
Both workflows use the same  file and reconciliation system.
Example Workflow
Start with your OpenAPI spec:
Create config file:
Run UIGen:
The reconciled spec (in memory) now has:
The UI won't show the  endpoint because it's ignored.
Advanced: Precedence Rules
When the same annotation exists in multiple places:
Config file (highest precedence)
Spec file
Default value (lowest precedence)
Example:
Testing
The reconciler is validated through:
Unit tests: Specific scenarios and edge cases
Property-based tests: 20 universal correctness properties, 100+ iterations each
Integration tests*: End-to-end with real OpenAPI/Swagger specs
Key properties verified:
Config precedence over spec annotations
Idempotence (applying twice = applying once)
Determinism (same input → same output)
Source spec non-mutation
Output validity preservation
Troubleshooting
Config not applied
Check:
Config file is named  (not )
Config file is in your project root
Element paths match your spec exactly
Server was restarted after config changes
Element path not found
Check:
HTTP method is uppercase:  not 
Path matches exactly:  not 
Schema name matches exactly:  not 
Validation errors
Check:
Config file has valid YAML syntax
Required fields present: , , , 
Annotation values are valid for their type
See Also
Spec Annotations - Available  annotations
How It Works - UIGen architecture overview
CLI Reference -  command options

title: Overview
content:
Supported Specs
UIGen supports the two most widely used API specification formats.
OpenAPI 3.x
OpenAPI 3.0 and 3.1 are the primary supported formats. All features are available:
Full  resolution including circular references
, , 
array for environment switching
with  type negotiation
, ,  schema composition (flattened to a single )
All standard security scheme types:  (bearer, basic), , 
Swagger 2.0
Swagger 2.0 (formerly OpenAPI 2.0) is fully supported. The adapter normalises Swagger 2.0 constructs to the same IR:
→ schema resolution
+  +  → server URL
→ auth schemes
/  → content type handling
parameters → request body schema
Format detection
UIGen auto-detects the spec format from the document's root keys:
→ OpenAPI 3.x adapter
→ Swagger 2.0 adapter
Both YAML and JSON are accepted.
Planned support
OpenAPI 3.1: full support is in progress. Most 3.1 documents work today; edge cases around JSON Schema vocabulary differences are being addressed.
GraphQL: planned for a future release.
Compatibility notes
UIGen aims to work with real-world specs, which are often imperfect. If your spec has issues, check  for details. See Adapters for how UIGen handles malformed or incomplete specs.

title: Overview
content:
Views & Components
UIGen generates a set of UI patterns (called views) from the operations in your spec. Each view is selected automatically based on the HTTP method, path shape, and request/response schema.
View types
View selection
The adapter assigns a  to each operation. The React renderer uses this hint to select the appropriate view component. You can inspect the assigned hints in .
Field components
Every view uses a set of field components to render individual form inputs and display values. See Field Components for the full list.
Customisation
All views can be replaced or augmented using the Override System. You can swap out an entire view, replace just the render output, or add side effects without changing the built-in rendering.

title: List View
content:
List View
The List View is generated for  endpoints (collection endpoints that return an array of records). It renders a full-featured data table using TanStack Table.
Features
Sorting: click any column header to sort ascending or descending
Pagination: automatic offset, cursor, or page-based pagination detected from query parameters
Row actions: Edit and Delete buttons on each row, linked to the corresponding edit form and delete confirmation
Navigation: clicking a row opens the Detail View for that record
Column generation: columns are derived from the response schema's top-level fields
Pagination detection
The adapter detects the pagination strategy from the query parameters defined in the spec:
If no pagination params are found, the table renders all returned records without pagination controls.
Example spec
This produces a paginated table with offset-based pagination controls.
Customisation
Replace the entire List View for a specific resource using the Override System:

title: Detail View
content:
Detail View
The Detail View is generated for  endpoints. It renders a read-only page showing all fields of a single record.
Features
Field rendering: all fields from the response schema are displayed with appropriate formatting
Related resource links: if the adapter detects relationships (e.g. ), links to related resources appear in the detail view
Edit button: links to the Edit Form View if a / endpoint exists for the resource
Delete button: triggers the delete confirmation dialog if a  endpoint exists
Field formatting
Fields are formatted based on their type and format:
Example spec
Customisation
Replace the Detail View for a specific resource:

title: Form View
content:
Form View
The Form View is generated for  endpoints. It renders a validated create form using React Hook Form and Zod.
Features
Field generation: form fields are derived from the  schema
Validation: constraints from the spec (, , , , , ) are enforced client-side via Zod
Field types: each field renders the appropriate input component based on its type and format
Submit handling: on success, navigates to the Detail View for the newly created record
Validation mapping
Wizard threshold
If the  schema has more than 8 fields, or contains nested objects, UIGen renders a Wizard View instead of a flat form.
Example spec
Customisation
Replace the Form View for a specific resource:

title: Edit Form View
content:
Edit Form View
The Edit Form View is generated for  and  endpoints. It works like the Form View but pre-populates all fields with the current record's values.
Features
Pre-population: fetches the current record via the corresponding  endpoint and fills all form fields
Partial updates:  endpoints produce a form where all fields are optional by default
Validation: same constraint mapping as the Form View
Cancel: navigates back to the Detail View without saving
Difference from Form View
Example spec
Customisation
Replace the Edit Form View for a specific resource:

title: Search View
content:
Search View
The Search View is generated for  endpoints that define query parameters for filtering. It renders a search bar and filter controls above a results table.
Features
Search bar: a text input that maps to the primary search query parameter
Filter controls: additional query parameters are rendered as filter inputs (dropdowns for enums, date pickers for date fields, etc.)
Live results: results update as the user types or changes filters
Results table: same table component as the List View, with sorting and pagination
Query parameter detection
The adapter identifies search/filter parameters by their location () and names. Parameters named , , , or  are treated as the primary search input. All other query parameters become secondary filter controls.
Example spec
This produces a search bar plus a status dropdown filter.
Customisation
Replace the Search View for a specific resource:

title: Dashboard View
content:
Dashboard View
The Dashboard View is the home page of the generated UI. It is auto-generated from the resources in your spec and provides an at-a-glance overview.
Features
Resource count widgets: each resource gets a card showing the total record count, fetched from the list endpoint
Quick links: each widget links to the resource's List View
Recent activity: if the spec includes timestamp fields, a recent activity feed is shown
Error resilience: if a count fetch fails, the widget shows a warning rather than crashing
Widget types
Customisation
The dashboard layout is driven by  in the IR:
You can replace the entire Dashboard View using the Override System:

title: Wizard View
content:
Wizard View
The Wizard View is a multi-step form triggered automatically when a  endpoint's request body has more than 8 fields, or contains nested objects. It groups fields into logical steps to reduce cognitive load.
Trigger conditions
UIGen uses the Wizard View instead of the flat Form View when either condition is met:
The  schema has more than 8 top-level fields
The  schema contains nested objects (fields with )
Step grouping
Steps are grouped automatically:
Required fields: all required fields appear in the first step
Optional fields: optional fields are grouped into subsequent steps by object nesting
Review: a final read-only review step shows all entered values before submission
Features
Progress indicator: shows the current step and total steps
Back / Next navigation: users can move between steps; earlier steps are validated before advancing
Per-step validation: only the fields in the current step are validated on Next
Review step: shows a summary of all entered values before final submission
Example spec
A  endpoint with a nested  object and more than 8 fields will automatically render as a wizard.
Customisation
Replace the Wizard View for a specific resource:

title: Login View
content:
Login View
The Login View is a credential-based login form. It is generated when an endpoint is annotated with  in the spec. This is used for APIs that require a username/password login to obtain a token.
When it appears
The Login View appears when:
The spec has a  entry requiring authentication
At least one endpoint is annotated with 
Without the annotation, UIGen renders a token input form instead (for Bearer/API Key schemes).
Features
Auto-generated fields: the form fields are derived from the login endpoint's  schema
Token extraction: after a successful login, UIGen extracts the token from the response using the  annotation
Session storage: the token is stored in session storage and injected into subsequent API requests via the proxy
Error handling: login errors are displayed inline
Example
See Credential Login for a full example with YAML annotations.
Customisation
Replace the Login View:

title: Field Components
content:
Field Components
Field components are the building blocks of forms and detail views. UIGen selects the appropriate component for each field based on its  and optional .
Field types
UI hints
You can override the default component selection using the  field in the IR, or via the  annotation (planned, see Planned Annotations):
Validation display
Validation errors are displayed inline below each field. The error message comes from the  if provided, otherwise a default message is generated from the rule type.
File uploads
File upload fields (, ) support drag-and-drop, file type validation, and size limits.
Basic file upload
This generates a file upload component with:
Drag-and-drop zone
Click to browse files
File preview after selection
Remove file button
File type restrictions
Use  to restrict allowed MIME types:
File size limits
Use  to set maximum file size in bytes:
Multiple file uploads
Use an array for multiple file uploads:
See File Upload Metadata for complete documentation.
Validation display
Validation errors are displayed inline below each field. The error message comes from the  if provided, otherwise a default message is generated from the rule type.
Customisation
Individual field components can be replaced using the Override System. See Override System for details.

title: Overview
content:
Authentication
UIGen auto-detects authentication requirements from the  section of your spec and generates the appropriate UI (no configuration required).
Auto-detection
When UIGen parses your spec, it reads  (OpenAPI 3.x) or  (Swagger 2.0) and builds an  in the IR. If  is true (i.e. the spec has a top-level  requirement), UIGen shows an authentication prompt before allowing access to the generated UI.
Supported schemes
Session storage
Credentials are stored in the browser's  under UIGen-specific keys. They are cleared when the browser tab is closed. This is intentional: UIGen is designed for development and internal tooling, not production user-facing apps.
Proxy injection
The CLI's built-in proxy reads credentials from UIGen-specific request headers and injects them into the forwarded request:
The UIGen-specific headers are stripped before the request reaches your API.
Multiple schemes
If your spec defines multiple security schemes, UIGen renders a scheme selector so the user can choose which one to use.

title: Bearer Token
content:
Bearer Token
UIGen auto-detects Bearer token authentication from  security schemes with .
Spec example
UI behaviour
When UIGen detects a Bearer scheme, it renders a token input form before the main UI. The user pastes their token and clicks Connect. The token is stored in  and injected into all subsequent API requests via the proxy as .
Swagger 2.0
In Swagger 2.0, Bearer tokens are typically modelled as an  in the  header. UIGen detects this pattern and renders the same token input form.

title: API Key
content:
API Key
UIGen auto-detects API Key authentication from  security schemes. The key can be sent in a header, query parameter, or cookie.
Spec example (header)
Spec example (query parameter)
UI behaviour
UIGen renders an API key input form. The user enters their key and clicks Connect. The key is stored in .
The proxy injects the key into forwarded requests:
*: added as a custom request header with the name from the scheme
: appended as a query parameter to the target URL
*: set as a  header (note: cookie injection has browser security limitations in some environments)
Swagger 2.0

title: HTTP Basic
content:
HTTP Basic
UIGen auto-detects HTTP Basic authentication from  security schemes with .
Spec example
UI behaviour
UIGen renders a username and password form. On submit, the credentials are Base64-encoded as  and stored in .
The proxy injects the credentials into forwarded requests as:
Security note
HTTP Basic credentials are stored in  and cleared when the tab is closed. UIGen is designed for development and internal tooling. Do not use it as a production authentication layer.
Swagger 2.0
Swagger 2.0 does not have a native  scheme type. Basic auth in Swagger 2.0 is typically modelled as:
UIGen detects this and renders the same username/password form.

title: Credential Login
content:
Credential Login
Some APIs require a login call to obtain a token (for example,  with a username and password that returns a JWT). UIGen supports this via the  annotation.
How it works
Annotate your login endpoint with 
Annotate the response field that contains the token with 
UIGen generates a login form from the request body schema
On successful login, UIGen extracts the token from the response and stores it in 
All subsequent requests are authenticated with the extracted token
Spec example (OpenAPI 3.x)
Spec example (Swagger 2.0)
Token path
The  annotation specifies the dot-notation path to the token in the response body. For example:
: 
: 
: 
Related
Authentication Overview
x-uigen-login annotation

title: Overview
content:
Spec Annotations
UIGen supports a set of  vendor extensions that let you customise the generated UI directly from your OpenAPI or Swagger spec. These annotations are optional (UIGen works without them) but they give you fine-grained control over labels, identifiers, and behaviour.
Available annotations
Where annotations are supported
Annotations can be placed at different levels of the spec:
Schema property: affects a single field
Schema object: affects all fields in a schema
Path item: affects all operations on a path
Operation: affects a single operation
Precedence
When the same annotation appears at multiple levels, the most specific level wins:
Swagger 2.0 support
All  annotations work in Swagger 2.0 documents in the same way as OpenAPI 3.x.

title: x-uigen-label
content:
x-uigen-label
The  annotation overrides the display label that UIGen generates for a field, operation, or resource. By default, UIGen derives labels from property names and operation summaries using title-case conversion.
Purpose
Use  when:
The property name is a technical identifier (e.g. , )
You want a more user-friendly label than the auto-generated one
You need to localise labels without changing the spec's property names
Supported locations
Resource-level label behavior
Single-operation resources: When a resource has only one operation, the operation label automatically applies to both the operation AND the resource.
Example:
Result: The "Me" resource in the sidebar shows as "My Profile"
Multi-operation resources: For resources with multiple operations, operation labels apply ONLY to the operations, not the resource.
Example:
Result: The "Templates" resource keeps its name, only the delete operation shows "Delete Template"
Explicit resource labels: To override a multi-operation resource name, use the base path without an HTTP method prefix:
Example:
Result: The resource shows as "Document Templates" in the sidebar
Precedence
When  appears at multiple levels, the most specific wins:
OpenAPI 3.x examples
Field label
Resource label
Operation label
Swagger 2.0 examples
Field label
Operation label
Default label generation
Without , UIGen generates labels by:
Splitting the property name on , , and camelCase boundaries
Title-casing each word
Joining with spaces
For example:  → ,  → .

title: x-uigen-id
content:
x-uigen-id
The  annotation overrides the stable identifier () that UIGen assigns to a resource or operation. This identifier is used by the Override System to target specific views for replacement.
Purpose
By default, UIGen generates  values from the resource slug and operation method. These IDs can change if you rename resources or restructure your spec. Use  to pin a stable identifier that won't change even if the spec evolves.
Default ID generation
Without , UIGen generates IDs using the pattern:
For example:
→ 
→ 
→ 
Fallback to slug
If  is not set, UIGen falls back to the auto-generated slug. The slug is derived from the path by stripping path parameters and joining segments with dots.
OpenAPI 3.x example
With these annotations, the Override System targets use the pinned IDs:
Swagger 2.0 example
Uniqueness
 values must be unique across all operations in the spec. If two operations share the same , UIGen logs a warning and uses the auto-generated ID for the conflicting operation.

title: x-uigen-ignore
content:
x-uigen-ignore
The  annotation allows you to exclude any element in your OpenAPI/Swagger spec from the generated UI. When an element is marked with , it is filtered out during IR construction and will not appear in any generated views, forms, or API configurations.
Purpose
Use  when:
You have internal or admin-only endpoints that should not appear in the public UI
You want to hide deprecated endpoints while keeping them in the spec
You have utility endpoints (health checks, metrics) that don't need UI representation
You want to exclude sensitive or internal fields from generated forms and views
You need to hide debug parameters or internal request/response structures
You want to exclude specific elements without removing them from the spec
How it works
 is a filtering mechanism that operates at the adapter layer, before elements reach the view hint classifier or relationship detector. When an element is marked with , the adapter stops processing at that node and does not evaluate any nested children (pruning behavior).
When all operations for a resource are ignored, the entire resource is excluded from the IR.
Supported values
 accepts boolean values only:
: exclude the operation from the generated UI
: include the operation explicitly (useful for overriding path-level annotations)
Non-boolean values (strings, numbers, objects, arrays, null) are treated as absent and a warning is logged.
Supported locations
 can be applied to any of the following OpenAPI/Swagger spec elements:
Pruning behavior
When an element is marked with , the adapter uses a pruning strategy: it stops processing at that node and does not evaluate any nested children or referenced schemas.
Example: If a schema object is ignored, none of its properties are processed:
Example: If a request body is ignored, its schema is not processed:
This pruning behavior improves performance by avoiding unnecessary processing of ignored subtrees.
Precedence
When  appears at multiple levels in the spec hierarchy, the most specific (child-level) annotation takes precedence. This allows you to override parent-level ignores for specific child elements.
Precedence hierarchy
Rule: Child annotations override parent annotations. An explicit  at the child level overrides  at the parent level, and vice versa.
Precedence examples
OpenAPI 3.x examples
Exclude a schema property
Exclude an entire schema object
Exclude a parameter
Exclude a request body
Exclude a response
Exclude a single operation
Exclude all operations on a path
Override path-level annotation for specific operation
Override schema-level annotation for specific property
Override path-level parameter for specific operation
Exclude entire resource
When all operations for a resource are ignored, the resource is automatically excluded:
Swagger 2.0 examples
Exclude a schema property
Exclude an entire schema object
Exclude a parameter
Exclude a response
Exclude a single operation
Exclude all operations on a path
Interaction with other annotations
 is checked before any other  annotations are processed. This means:
With 
If an operation has both  and , the operation is excluded from the IR and does not appear in :
With 
If an operation has both  and , the operation is excluded and the label is never applied:
With 
If an operation has both  and , the operation is excluded and the ID is never used:
Relationship detection
When a resource is excluded because all its operations are ignored, no other resource will have relationship links pointing to it. This is automatic (the relationship detector only operates on resources that appear in the IR).
Default behavior
Without , all operations are included in the generated UI (default behavior). The annotation is entirely optional.
Validation
The adapter validates  values and provides feedback:
Non-boolean value: Warning logged: . Operation is included (treated as absent).
All operations ignored: Warning logged: . Empty resources array returned.
Operation ignored: Info logged: .

title: x-uigen-ref
content:
x-uigen-ref
The  annotation explicitly declares that a schema field references another resource. It provides full control over how the relationship is resolved and displayed, overriding any auto-detected relationship heuristics.
Purpose
Use  when:
A field stores a reference to another resource (e.g.  stores a user ID)
You want forms to render a select/autocomplete widget instead of a plain text input
You want list and detail views to display human-readable labels instead of raw IDs
You need to override auto-detected relationships with explicit configuration
Annotation shape
Field reference
OpenAPI 3.x examples
Basic reference
Template label
With filter
Swagger 2.0 examples
Basic reference
Template label
Behavior
Forms
When a field has , UIGen renders it as a select or autocomplete widget:
Fetches options from the  endpoint
Appends  parameters as query params (if provided)
Maps each record to an option where:
Value = 
Label = resolved  template
On form submit, sends the  value (not the display label)
List views
In list views, UIGen resolves and displays the label for each row:
Fetches records from the  endpoint
Matches each stored value against 
Displays the resolved  template for matching records
Falls back to the raw stored value if no match is found
Detail views
In detail views, UIGen resolves and displays the label:
Fetches the referenced record from the  endpoint
Displays the resolved  template
Falls back to the raw stored value if the fetch fails
Graceful degradation
When the referenced resource cannot be fetched (network error, 4xx, 5xx):
Forms: fall back to a plain text input
List/Detail views: display the raw stored value
Dev mode: emit a  with the resource path and error reason
UIGen never crashes due to a failed  fetch.
Template syntax
The  supports two formats:
Plain field
Returns the value of the  field from the record.
Template with placeholders
Replaces each  placeholder with the corresponding field value from the record. Missing or null fields are replaced with empty strings.
Examples:
→ 
→ 
→ 
Precedence over auto-detection
When  is present on a field, UIGen does not apply auto-detected relationship heuristics (e.g.  field name patterns). The explicit annotation always wins.
Fields without  continue to use auto-detection.

title: File Upload Metadata
content:
File Upload Metadata
UIGen provides vendor extensions to control file upload validation and behavior. These annotations work with  and  fields in your OpenAPI spec.
Available annotations
Restricts which file types users can upload. Accepts an array of MIME type strings.
OpenAPI 3.x example
Swagger 2.0 example
Common MIME types
Images:
, , , , 
Documents:
(DOC)
(DOCX)
(XLS)
(XLSX)
Video:
, , 
Audio:
, , 
Archives:
, , 
Wildcard patterns
Use wildcards to accept entire categories:
Sets the maximum allowed file size in bytes. Files exceeding this limit are rejected with a validation error.
Example
Common size values
Validation behavior
When a user selects a file that exceeds the limit:
The file is rejected before upload
An error message displays: "File size exceeds maximum of X MB"
The form cannot be submitted until a valid file is selected
Combining both annotations
Use both annotations together for complete control:
Multiple file uploads
For array fields, the size limit applies to each individual file:
Standard OpenAPI properties
UIGen also respects the standard  property from OpenAPI 3.x:
When both  and  are present,  takes precedence.
File type detection
UIGen automatically categorizes files into types for optimized validation and preview:
This categorization is automatic and does not require any annotations.
Validation errors
UIGen performs client-side validation before upload:
File type mismatch:
File too large:
Empty file:
Extension mismatch:
Browser compatibility
The file type restrictions use the HTML  attribute, which is supported in all modern browsers. However, users can still attempt to select invalid files, so server-side validation is still required.
Config Reconciliation
You can override file metadata annotations using the Config Reconciliation system without modifying your OpenAPI spec.
Example config file
Create a  file in your project root:
Config precedence
When the same annotation exists in both your spec and config file, the config value takes precedence:
Using the Config GUI
The Config GUI provides a visual interface for managing file metadata annotations:
Start the config GUI: 
Select a file field in the visual editor
Configure  using the multi-select dropdown
Set  using the file size input with unit selector
Save your changes to 
The config GUI automatically filters annotations to show only those applicable to file fields.
See also
Field Components - File upload component behavior
Spec Annotations Overview - All available annotations
Config Reconciliation - Override annotations without modifying your spec

title: Overview
content:
Override System
> In development: the Override System is implemented and available in the current release, but the API may change in minor versions until it stabilises.
The Override System lets you selectively replace or augment any auto-generated view without touching the rest of the UI. Think of it like CSS selectors for components: you target a specific view by its ID and provide a replacement or enhancement.
Mental model
Just as CSS lets you target a specific element and override its styles, the Override System lets you target a specific view by its  and override its rendering. Everything else continues to work as generated.
Three modes
Priority order
When multiple overrides target the same view, the most specific wins:
Targeting views
Each view has a stable  that you use to target it. The ID follows the pattern :
You can also pin custom IDs using the [](/docs/spec-annotations/x-uigen-id) annotation.
See Override ID Addressing for the full addressing scheme.
Registration
Overrides are registered before the app mounts:

title: Component Mode
content:
Component Mode
Component mode replaces the entire auto-generated view with your own React component. UIGen renders your component instead of the built-in view (you have full control over the UI).
When to use
Use component mode when:
The built-in view doesn't fit your design requirements
You need a completely custom layout or interaction pattern
You want to use a third-party component library for a specific view
Example
Props
Your component receives the following props:
Registration
Call  before the UIGen app mounts. Typically this is done in your app's entry point, before rendering the  component.
Multiple overrides
You can register overrides for multiple views:

title: Render Mode
content:
Render Mode
Render mode lets you replace only the rendered output of a view. UIGen still handles data fetching, loading states, and pagination (you just provide the JSX to render).
When to use
Use render mode when:
You want a custom layout but don't want to re-implement data fetching
You need to change how data is displayed without changing the data source
You want to keep UIGen's loading and error states but customise the success state
Example
Render props
The  function receives:
Difference from component mode

title: Use Hooks Mode
content:
Use Hooks Mode
Use hooks mode lets you add side effects to a view without replacing the built-in rendering. UIGen renders the view as normal (your hook runs alongside it).
When to use
Use hooks mode when:
You want to track analytics events when a view is visited
You need to update  based on the current resource
You want to trigger a side effect (e.g. refresh a badge count) when a view loads
You need to integrate with an external system without changing the UI
Important
The built-in view still renders in hooks mode. You are not replacing anything (you are adding behaviour alongside the existing view).
Example: analytics tracking
Example: document title
Hook props
The  function receives:
Rules
You can use any React hooks inside 
Do not return JSX (the return value is ignored)
The hook runs on every render of the view, following normal React rules

title: Override ID Addressing
content:
Override ID Addressing
Every view in UIGen has a stable  that you use to target it with the Override System. This page documents the full addressing scheme.
Default ID pattern
Auto-generated IDs follow the pattern:
Full addressing table
Resource slug derivation
The resource slug is derived from the path by:
Taking the first path segment after  (e.g.  → )
Lowercasing and replacing non-alphanumeric characters with 
For example:
→

Custom IDs
Pin a custom ID using the [](/docs/spec-annotations/x-uigen-id) annotation:
Then target it in the Override System:
Wildcard targeting
You can target all views for a resource using a wildcard (planned, not yet available):
Inspecting IDs
To see the  for each view, inspect  in the browser console:

title: serve
content:
uigen serve
The  command reads an API spec and starts a local server with a fully generated UI.
Usage
 can be a local file path or a remote URL.
Options
Examples
Local file
Remote URL
Custom port
Custom proxy base
Override the API target when it differs from the spec's  field:
Verbose proxy logging
With , the CLI logs each proxied request and response:
Serving modes
The CLI operates in two modes depending on how it was installed:
Dev mode
Used when running from the monorepo (no pre-built renderer in ). Starts a Vite dev server with hot module replacement.
Static mode
Used when installed via npm or npx. Serves the pre-built  directory with a plain Node.js HTTP server. Vite is not required at runtime.
Both modes expose the same URL and behaviour.
Renderer support
Currently only  is available.  and  renderers are planned for a future release. Passing an unknown renderer value falls back to  with a warning.
Notes
The spec file is read once at startup. Changes to the spec require restarting the server.
The proxy forwards all requests to  to the target server.
Authentication headers are injected by the proxy transparently (your API never sees UIGen-specific headers).

title: Overview
content:
Extending UIGen
UIGen is designed to be extended. The core IR is framework-agnostic, which means you can build a renderer for any UI framework (Svelte, Vue, Angular, or anything else) using  as the only required dependency.
Architecture
The extension point is the IR. Your renderer:
Receives the IR from  (injected by the CLI)
Reads , , , and 
Renders the appropriate UI for each resource and operation
The CLI handles spec parsing, IR injection, and the API proxy. Your renderer only needs to handle the UI layer.
Required dependency
 provides the TypeScript types for the IR, which you need to build a type-safe renderer.
Minimal renderer example
Serving your renderer
Pass your renderer's entry point to the CLI using :
The CLI looks for  in . Your renderer package must export a  that the CLI can serve.
Planned renderer packages
*: Svelte renderer (planned, Phase 4)
*: Vue 3 renderer (planned, Phase 4)
See the Roadmap for the timeline.
Community renderers
If you build a renderer for UIGen, open a pull request to add it to this page.

title: Roadmap
content:
Roadmap
UIGen is developed in phases. Here's what's been shipped and what's coming next.
Phase 1: Core vertical slice ✅ Complete
[x] Monorepo scaffold (pnpm + Vite + TypeScript)
[x] IR types (auth, relationships, pagination hints, validation rules)
[x] OpenAPI 3.x adapter with full  resolution
[x] Swagger 2.0 adapter
[x] View hint classifier (list, detail, create, update, delete, search, wizard, action)
[x] Relationship detector (hasMany / belongsTo)
[x] Pagination detector (offset, cursor, page-based)
[x] Core field components (TextField, NumberField, SelectField, DatePicker, FileUpload, ArrayField, ObjectField)
[x] ListView with TanStack Table: sorting, pagination, filtering, row actions
[x] FormView with React Hook Form + Zod validation
[x] CLI  command with Vite proxy
[x] Opinionated theme (shadcn/ui dark/light toggle)
[x] Authentication UI (Bearer token + API Key)
[x] Environment switching (server dropdown from spec )
[x] Error resilience (graceful degradation, error boundary, toast notifications)
Phase 2: Full surface area ✅ Complete
[x] DetailView with related resource links
[x] EditFormView (pre-populated from current record)
[x] Delete with confirmation dialog
[x] Custom action buttons (non-CRUD operations)
[x] SearchView (global + per-resource filters)
[x] DashboardView (auto-generated overview with resource counts)
[x] WizardView (multi-step for large forms)
[x] Sidebar layout + TopBar + Breadcrumbs + responsive shell
[x] React Router with full URL navigation and browser history
[x] Config reconciliation system (runtime annotation merging from )
Phase 3: Extension & distribution 🔜 In progress
[ ]  vendor extension support (widget, label, hidden, order, view)
[ ] : theme/behaviour/resource overrides
[ ] : spec linting with actionable errors and line numbers
[ ] : static production build output
[ ] OAuth2 PKCE authentication flow
[ ] Spec hot-reloading (file watcher → WebSocket push to UI)
[ ] Loading skeletons with shimmer animation
[ ] Virtual scrolling for large datasets (TanStack Virtual)
[ ] Request / response interceptors (config-driven middleware)
[ ] Response transformation (JSONPath + JS functions)
[ ] Publish all packages to npm (, , )
Phase 4: Renderer ecosystem
[ ] : Svelte renderer consuming the same IR
[ ] : Vue 3 renderer
[ ] Plugin API: register custom adapters, field types, and view strategies as npm packages
[ ] : visual configuration dashboard
[ ] : chart widgets from numeric data
[ ] : map renderer for geo coordinate fields
[ ] GraphQL adapter
[ ] OpenAPI 3.1 full support

title: Contributing
content:
Contributing
UIGen is open source and contributions are welcome. This page covers how to set up the monorepo, run tests, and submit changes.
Monorepo setup
UIGen uses pnpm workspaces. You need Node.js 18+ and pnpm 9+.
Project structure
See ARCHITECTURE.md for a detailed breakdown of the architecture and design decisions.
Running tests
Tests use Vitest and fast-check for property-based testing.
Making changes
Create a branch from 
Make your changes
Add or update tests
Run  to verify everything passes
Create a changeset (see below)
Open a pull request
Changesets
UIGen uses Changesets to manage versions and changelogs. When you make a change that should be released, create a changeset:
This prompts you to:
Select which packages have changed
Choose the version bump type (major, minor, patch)
Write a summary of the changes
The changeset file is committed with your PR. See RELEASING.md for the full release process.
Version bump guidelines
Code style
TypeScript strict mode is enabled in all packages
ESLint is configured at the workspace root
No  types without a comment explaining why
Tests must pass before merging
Questions
Open an issue on GitHub if you have questions or want to discuss a change before implementing it.
